en.extract:
The Italian sparrow (Passer italiae), also known as the cisalpine sparrow, is a passerine bird of the sparrow family Passeridae, found in Italy and other parts of the Mediterranean region. In appearance, it is intermediate between the house sparrow, and the Spanish sparrow, a species of the Mediterranean and Central Asia closely related to the house sparrow. The Italian sparrow occurs in northern Italy and neighbouring regions, with intermediates with the house sparrow in a very narrow contact zone in the Alps, a slow gradation in appearance from the Italian to Spanish sparrows across central and southern Italy, and more birds of intermediate appearance in Malta, Crete, and other parts of the Mediterranean.
There has been much debate on the origins and taxonomic status of the Italian sparrow, especially given its possible hybrid origin. Some have classified it as a subspecies of the house sparrow, a subspecies of the Spanish sparrow, or as a separate species, a treatment followed if only for convenience by authorities such as the Handbook of the Birds of the World. A DNA analysis by Glenn-Peter Sætre and colleagues published in 2011 indicated an origin of the Italian sparrow through hybridisation between the Spanish and house sparrows, and Sætre and colleagues argued that given its origins and the limited extent of hybridisation, the treatment as a distinct species was supported. The Italian sparrow is unofficially considered the national bird of Italy.


Description
The Italian sparrow is a small chunky bird, with grey and brown plumage. The sexes differ in their plumage pattern, and slightly in length. The male has a head patterned like that of the Spanish sparrow, with a chestnut crown, nape and sides of head, and white cheeks. The male's upperparts are bright chestnut, and its underparts are pale grey, lacking the black streaking of the Spanish sparrow. The male has a black patch on its throat and chest, known as a bib or badge. This patch, like much of the male's plumage is dull in fresh non-breeding plumage and is brightened by wear and preening. The female is nearly identical to the female house sparrow, but it differs from the female Spanish sparrow in its lack of black streaks on the underparts. Albinism is occasionally recorded.

The Italian sparrow is about the same size as the house sparrow at 14–16 centimetres (5.5–6.3 in) in length. The tail is 5.3–6 centimetres (2.1–2.4 in), the tarsus 18.6–21 millimetres (0.73–0.83 in), and wing lengths for males are 7.3–8.2 centimetres (2.9–3.2 in). The Italian sparrow's weight varies seasonally from 30 grams (1.1 oz) in the winter to 26 grams (0.92 oz) in the summer.
The vocalisations of the Italian sparrow are similar to those of both the Spanish sparrow and the house sparrow. Its vocalisations carry better in natural environments than those of the house sparrow. The male gives a chreep call like that of the Spanish sparrow to proclaim nest ownership, and a faster version of this as part of courtship display. Male song patterns grade slowly into those of the Spanish sparrow across southern Italy, but in the area of overlap between the house and Italian sparrows, the two birds sound alike.


Taxonomy
The taxonomic status of the Italian sparrow has been a matter of debate. It has been variously regarded as a stable hybrid between the house sparrow and Spanish sparrow, or as a subspecies of either the house or Spanish sparrow. Many authorities, including the Handbook of the Birds of the World, recognise it as a separate species, if only for convenience. Others, including many conservation groups, consider the Italian sparrow a simple hybrid and ignore it. The chromosomes of Italian sparrows are distinct from those of the house sparrow, but mitochondrial DNA suggest a close relation to the house sparrow.
A DNA analysis by Jo Hermansen, Glenn-Peter Sætre, and a group of other scientists from Norway published in Molecular Ecology in 2011 indicates that the Italian sparrow originated as a hybrid between house and Spanish sparrows. It has mitochondrial DNA from both parent species. Additionally, it is now breeding beside the Spanish sparrow without cross-breeding in areas where the species both occur. Although it hybridises with the house sparrow in a sparsely populated contact zone in the Alps, the contact zone is characterized by relatively abrupt changes in species-specific male plumage, suggesting that partial reproductive isolation based on plumage may also have developed between these two taxa. As a genetically distinct group that is reproductively isolated from the parental species, it must be recognised as a separate species, according to Hermansen and colleagues.


Distribution and habitat
The Italian sparrow is found in northern and central Italy, Corsica, and small parts of France, Switzerland, Austria, and Slovenia. Its distribution was described by Italian zoologist Enrico Hillyer Giglioli in 1881 as professing "'Conservative opinions'; for it keeps strictly within our current political frontiers". At the northernmost edge of its range in the southern Alps, there is a narrow hybrid zone about 20–30 km (12–19 mi) wide with the house sparrow. In southern Italy, there is a gradual clinal trend with the Spanish sparrow, with birds increasing in their similarity to the Spanish sparrow in appearance and ecology further south, from around Naples to western Sicily, where birds resemble pure Spanish sparrows. However, this trend may be superficial, and the Handbook of the Birds of the World recognises birds from Sicily and Crete as Italian sparrows.
Sardinia is occupied by Spanish sparrows, while sparrows on Malta, Crete, and the adjacent islands are intermediates similar to the Italian sparrow. On Malta, sparrows resemble the Spanish sparrow, with urban birds behaving much like the house sparrow, and rural birds like the Spanish sparrow. The situation is complicated by house and Spanish sparrows which winter and migrate on Malta. A more complex situation occurs in parts of northern Africa, where a highly variable mixed, interbreeding population of house sparrows and Spanish sparrows occurs. This hybrid swarm shows a full range of characters from nearly pure house sparrows to nearly pure Spanish sparrows and everything between.
The Italian sparrow is associated with human habitations, inhabiting towns, cities, and agricultural areas. In most cities in Italy, it shares the urban environment with the Eurasian tree sparrow, and in some parts of Naples, it is replaced entirely by this species.
The Italian sparrow's breeding population is believed to comprise 5 to 10 million pairs, 750,000 to 900,000 of which are estimated to live in urban areas. It has a population density of 58 to 160 pairs per square kilometer. Up to the mid-1990s, its population increased steadily, probably due to increased urbanisation. Between 2000 and 2005, the Italian sparrow's population in Italy declined by 27.1 percent, mirroring the declines of the house sparrow throughout Europe. From 1998 to 2008, urban populations declined by about 50 percent. A study of the Italian sparrow's status listed a large number of potential causes for the Italian sparrow's decline, including shortages of insect food, agricultural intensification, and reductions of green areas. The Italian sparrow is among the most common birds in Italian cities, but other species, including the European goldfinch, are more common.


Behaviour
The Italian sparrow's behaviour is similar in many ways to that of the house sparrow. It is a social bird, which feeds mostly on seeds and insects.
It is mostly sedentary, but it wanders to some extent outside its breeding season. These wanderings are mostly local, but they may extend into southern France. Similarly, the house sparrow sometimes occurs as a winter visitor in northern Italy.
Besides intergrading with the Spanish and house sparrows, the Italian sparrow has been recorded hybridising with the Eurasian tree sparrow. The eggs of the Italian sparrow do not seem to differ from those of the house sparrow. Broods may contain two to eight eggs, with an average of about 5.2.


Relationships with humans
In most of its range, the Italian sparrow is an abundant and familiar bird of houses. It has been one of the wild birds most commonly consumed as food in Italy. Portrayals of the Italian sparrow or one of its relatives have been found at Pompeii. Like the house sparrow, the Italian sparrow is considered a biological indicator of pollution.


References

Works cited

The '''Italian sparrow''' ('''''Passer italiae'''''), also known as the '''cisalpine sparrow''', is a [[passerine]] bird of the [[Old World sparrow|sparrow]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] Passeridae, found in Italy and other parts of the [[Mediterranean region]]. In appearance, it is intermediate between the [[house sparrow]], and the [[Spanish sparrow]], a species of the Mediterranean and Central Asia closely related to the house sparrow. The Italian sparrow occurs in northern Italy and neighbouring regions, with intermediates with the house sparrow in a very narrow contact zone in the [[Alps]], a slow gradation in appearance from the Italian to Spanish sparrows across central and southern Italy, and more birds of intermediate appearance in [[Malta]], [[Crete]], and other parts of the Mediterranean.

There has been much debate on the origins and taxonomic status of the Italian sparrow, especially given its possible hybrid origin. Some have classified it as a subspecies of the house sparrow, a subspecies of the Spanish sparrow, or as a separate species, a treatment followed if only for convenience by authorities such as the ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]''. A DNA analysis by Glenn-Peter Sætre and colleagues published in 2011 indicated an origin of the Italian sparrow through hybridisation between the Spanish and house sparrows, and Sætre and colleagues argued that given its origins and the limited extent of hybridisation, the treatment as a distinct species was supported. The Italian sparrow is unofficially considered the [[national bird]] of Italy.<ref>{{cite web|title=Passero Italiano: L'uccello nazionale d'Italia|date=18 December 2022 |url=https://www.concaternanaoggi.it/passero-italiano-luccello-nazionale-ditalia/|publisher=Conca Ternana Oggi|access-date=22 August 2024|language=it}}</ref>

== Description ==
The Italian sparrow is a small chunky bird, with grey and brown [[plumage]]. The sexes differ in their plumage pattern, and slightly in length.<ref name="Töpfer">{{cite journal|last=Töpfer|first=Till|year=2006|title=The taxonomic status of the Italian Sparrow – ''Passer italiae'' (Vieillot 1817): Speciation by stabilised hybridisation? A critical analysis|journal=Zootaxa|volume=1325|pages=117–145|doi=10.11646/zootaxa.1325.1.8|issn=1175-5334|url=http://www.mapress.com/zootaxa/2006f/z01325p145f.pdf|format=PDF, abstract only}}</ref> The male has a head patterned like that of the [[Spanish sparrow]], with a chestnut [[crown (anatomy)|crown]], nape and sides of head, and white cheeks. The male's upperparts are bright chestnut, and its underparts are pale grey, lacking the black streaking of the Spanish sparrow.<ref name="Summers164–165"/> The male has a black patch on its throat and chest, known as a bib or badge. This patch, like much of the male's plumage is dull in fresh non-breeding plumage and is brightened by wear and preening.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Bogliani, G. |author2=Brangi, A.|year=1990|title=Abrasion of the status badge in the male Italian Sparrow ''Passer italiae''|journal=Bird Study|volume=37|issue=3|pages=195–198|doi=10.1080/00063659009477057|doi-access=free|bibcode=1990BirdS..37..195B }}</ref> The female is nearly identical to the female [[house sparrow]], but it differs from the female Spanish sparrow in its lack of black streaks on the underparts.<ref name="Summers164–165"/> [[Albinism]] is occasionally recorded.<ref name="Modena"/>

| image1            = Italian Sparrow (35511779721).jpg
| total_width       = 480

| align             = center
| caption2          = Female in [[Piedmont]] (Italy)
| caption1          = Male in [[Corsica]] (France)
}}

The Italian sparrow is about the same size as the house sparrow at {{convert|14|–|16|cm|in}} in length.<ref name="Backhouse">{{harvnb|Backhouse|1890|pp=83–84}}</ref> The tail is {{convert|5.3|–|6|cm|in}}, the [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] {{convert|18.6|–|21|mm|in}},<ref name="Backhouse"/><ref>{{harvnb|Cramp|Perrins|1994|p=319}}</ref> and wing lengths for males are {{convert|7.3|–|8.2|cm|in}}.<ref name="Summers164–165">{{harvnb|Summers-Smith|1988| pp=164–165}}</ref> The Italian sparrow's weight varies seasonally from {{convert|30|g|oz}} in the winter to {{convert|26|g|oz}} in the summer.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Fulgione, D. |author2=Rusch, C. E. |author3=Esposito, A. |author4=Milone, M. |year=1998|title=Dynamics of weight, fat and moult in the Italian Sparrow ''Passer domesticus italiae''|journal=Acta Ornithologica|volume=33|pages=93–98|url=http://psjc.icm.edu.pl/psjc/cgi-bin/getdoc.cgi?B99-5-114|access-date=2010-04-22}}</ref>

The [[bird vocalization|vocalisations]] of the Italian sparrow are similar to those of both the Spanish sparrow and the house sparrow. Its vocalisations carry better in natural environments than those of the house sparrow. The male gives a ''chreep'' call like that of the Spanish sparrow to proclaim nest ownership, and a faster version of this as part of [[courtship display]]. Male song patterns grade slowly into those of the Spanish sparrow across southern Italy, but in the area of overlap between the house and Italian sparrows, the two birds sound alike.<ref name="HBW"/><ref>{{cite journal|url=http://www.ciso-coi.org/avoxvol24.htm|volume=24|issue=2|date=December 2000|title=Song clinal variability in ''Passer italiae'', a species of probable hybrid origins|pages=107–112|journal=Avocetta|access-date=2010-04-20|author1=Fulgione, D.|author2=Esposito, A.|author3=Rusch, C. E.|author4=Milone, M.|archive-date=2010-05-07|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100507000753/http://www.ciso-coi.org/avoxvol24.htm|url-status=usurped}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The [[Taxonomy (biology)|taxonomic]] status of the Italian sparrow has been a matter of debate.<ref name="Töpfer"/><ref name=bbc>{{cite web|last=Gill|first=Victoria|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/nature/14947902|archive-url=https://archive.today/20120719014433/http://www.bbc.co.uk/nature/14947902|url-status=dead|archive-date=July 19, 2012|title=Italian sparrow joins family as a new species|access-date=2011-09-19|work=BBC News}}</ref> It has been variously regarded as a stable [[Hybrid (biology)|hybrid]] between the house sparrow and Spanish sparrow,<ref>{{cite journal|last=Stephan|first=B.|year=1986|title=Die Evolutionstheorie und der taxonomische Status des Italiensperlings|journal=Mitteilungen aus dem Zoologischen Museum in Berlin|volume=62|doi=10.1515/9783112567302-003 |language=de}}</ref> or as a [[subspecies]] of either the house<ref name=bwp>{{harvnb|Snow|Perrins|1998|p=1509}}</ref> or Spanish sparrow.<ref name="Töpfer"/><ref name="Summers162–163">{{Harvnb|Summers-Smith|1988|pp=162–163}}</ref> Many authorities, including the ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'', recognise it as a separate species, if only for convenience.<ref name="HBW">{{cite book|first=J. Denis|last=Summers-Smith|editor1=del Hoyo, Josep |editor2=Elliott, Andrew |editor3=Christie, David|contribution=Family Passeridae (Old World Sparrows)|title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |volume=14: Bush-shrikes to Old World Sparrows|year=2009|location=Barcelona|publisher=Lynx Edicions|isbn=978-84-96553-50-7|title-link=Handbook of the Birds of the World}}</ref><ref name="AERC">{{cite web|url=http://www.aerc.eu/DOCS/AERCTACPending.pdf |title=Pending decisions with comments on their progress |publisher=AERC TAC |access-date=2010-08-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110720153056/http://www.aerc.eu/DOCS/AERCTACPending.pdf |archive-date=20 July 2011 |url-status=dead }}</ref> Others, including many conservation groups, consider the Italian sparrow a simple hybrid and ignore it.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Massa|first=Bruno|year=2006|title=Biological significance and conservation of biogeographical bird populations as shown by selected Mediterranean species|journal=Avocetta|volume=30|pages=5–14|url=http://www.ornitologiasiciliana.it/pdf/BMassaBirdConservation.pdf|access-date=2010-05-03|archive-date=2011-07-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722043756/http://www.ornitologiasiciliana.it/pdf/BMassaBirdConservation.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref> The [[chromosome]]s of Italian sparrows are distinct from those of the house sparrow,<ref name="AERC"/> but [[mitochondrial DNA]] suggest a close relation to the house sparrow.<ref>{{cite 2001JMolE..53..144A|citeseerx= 10.1.1.520.4878|s2cid= 21782750}}</ref>

A DNA analysis by Jo Hermansen, Glenn-Peter Sætre, and a group of other scientists from Norway published in ''[[Molecular Ecology]]'' in 2011 indicates that the Italian sparrow originated as a hybrid between house and Spanish sparrows. It has [[mitochondrial DNA]] from both parent species. Additionally, it is now breeding beside the Spanish sparrow without cross-breeding in areas where the species both occur.<ref name=bbc /><ref name=species/> Although it hybridises with the house sparrow in a sparsely populated contact zone in the Alps, the contact zone is characterized by relatively abrupt changes in species-specific male plumage, suggesting that partial reproductive isolation based on plumage may also have developed between these two taxa.<ref name=species /> As a genetically distinct group that is reproductively isolated from the parental species, it must be recognised as a separate species, according to Hermansen and colleagues.<ref name=bbc /><ref name=species>{{cite journal|last1=Hermansen|first1= Jo S. |first2=Stein A.|last2=Sæther|first3=Tore O. |last3=Elgvin|first4=Thomas |last4=Borge|first5= Elin|first6= Glenn-Peter |last6=Sætre|date=September 2011| title=Hybrid speciation in sparrows I: phenotypic intermediacy, genetic admixture and barriers to gene flow | journal= [[Molecular Ecology]] | volume=20| issue= 18 | pages=3812–3822|doi=10.1111/j.1365-294X.2011.05183.x |pmid= 21771138 |last5=Hjelle |bibcode= 2011MolEc..20.3812H |s2cid= 24177994 }}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Italian sparrow is found in northern and central Italy, [[Corsica]], and small parts of France, Switzerland, Austria, and Slovenia.<ref name="Töpfer"/> Its distribution was described by Italian zoologist [[Enrico Hillyer Giglioli]] in 1881 as professing "'Conservative opinions'; for it keeps strictly within our current political frontiers".<ref>{{cite journal|last=Giglioli|first=Henry Hillyer|year=1881|title=Notes on the Avifauna of Italy|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16251462|journal=The Ibis|series=4|volume=5|issue=XVIII|pages = 181–222}}</ref> At the northernmost edge of its range in the southern [[Alps]], there is a narrow hybrid zone about {{convert|20|–|30|km|mi|abbr=on}} wide with the house sparrow.<ref name="SummersDD">{{harvnb|Summers-Smith|1988| pp=122–126}}</ref> In southern Italy, there is a gradual [[Cline (biology)|clinal]] trend with the Spanish sparrow, with birds increasing in their similarity to the Spanish sparrow in appearance and ecology further south, from around [[Naples]] to western [[Sicily]], where birds resemble pure Spanish sparrows.<ref name="Töpfer"/><ref name="Summers162–163"/> However, this trend may be superficial,<ref>{{cite book|title=On the origin of an avian species by means of natural hybridization|last=Hermansen|first=Jo Skeie|year=2009|url=http://www.duo.uio.no/sok/work.html?WORKID=99094&lang=en|access-date=2010-10-23|location=Oslo|publisher=Universitetet i Oslo|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718172428/http://www.duo.uio.no/sok/work.html?WORKID=99094&lang=en|archive-date=2011-07-18|url-status=dead}}</ref> and the ''Handbook of the Birds of the World'' recognises birds from Sicily and [[Crete]] as Italian sparrows.<ref name="HBW"/>

[[Sardinia]] is occupied by Spanish sparrows, while sparrows on [[Malta]], Crete, and the adjacent islands are intermediates similar to the Italian sparrow.<ref name="Töpfer"/><ref name="search 22–23"/><ref>{{cite journal|last=Meinertzhagen|first=R.|author-link=Richard Meinertzhagen|title=A Note on the Breeding Birds of Crete|journal=The Ibis|year=1921|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8759834|series=11|volume=3}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Despott|first=G.|title=Notes on the Ornithology of Malta|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8764408|journal=The Ibis|series=10|volume=5|issue=3|pages=281–349|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1917.tb00557.x|year=2008}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Hartert|first=E.|title=Types of Birds in the Tring Museum. B. Types in the General Collection |journal=Novitates Zoologicae|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3858226|year=1919|volume=XXVI|pages=123–178}}</ref> On Malta, sparrows resemble the Spanish sparrow, with urban birds behaving much like the house sparrow, and rural birds like the Spanish sparrow. The situation is complicated by house and Spanish sparrows which winter and migrate on Malta.<ref>{{harvnb|Summers-Smith|1988| pp=35–36}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title=Ein neues Ereignis: Wandernde Weidensperlinge ''Passer hispaniolensis'' in Malta|last=Sultana|first=Joe|year=2007|journal=Ornithologische Mitteilungen |volume=59 |issue=1 |pages=30–32|language=de}}</ref><ref>{{harvnb|Roberts|1954|pp=150–152}}</ref> A more complex situation occurs in parts of northern Africa, where a highly variable mixed, interbreeding population of house sparrows and Spanish sparrows occurs. This [[hybrid swarm]] shows a full range of characters from nearly pure house sparrows to nearly pure Spanish sparrows and everything between.<ref name=bwp/><ref name="search 22–23">{{Harvnb|Summers-Smith|1992|pp=22–23}}</ref><ref>{{Harvnb|Summers-Smith|1988|pp=126, 166}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Rothschild|first1=W.|author-link=Walter Rothschild|last2=Hartert|first2=E.|author-link2=Ernst Hartert|title=Ornithological Explorations in Algeria|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3109742#480|journal=[[Novitates Zoologicae]]|year=1911|volume=18|pages=456–550|doi=10.5962/bhl.part.1696|doi-access=free}}</ref>

The Italian sparrow is associated with human habitations, inhabiting towns, cities, and agricultural areas. In most cities in Italy, it shares the urban environment with the [[Eurasian tree sparrow]],<ref name="Summers162–163"/><ref>{{cite journal|title=Notes on the Birds observed at Pisa and in its Neighbourhood during the Winter, Spring, and Summer of 1864|author=Giglioli, Henry|year=1865|journal=The Ibis|series=New Series|volume=1|pages=50–63|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/31142583|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1865.tb05751.x}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Wallis, H. M.|title=Notes upon the Northern Limit of the Italian Sparrow (''Passer italiae'')|journal=The Ibis|series=5|volume=5|issue=4|pages=454–455|year=1887|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8521966|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1887.tb06627.x}}</ref> and in some parts of [[Naples]], it is replaced entirely by this species.<ref>{{harvnb|Summers-Smith|1992| p=25}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Mirabella|first=P.|author2=Fraissinet, M. |author3= Milone, M. |title=Breeding birds and territorial heterogeneity in Naples city (Italy)|journal=Acta Ornithologica|year=1996|url=http://psjc.icm.edu.pl/psjc/cgi-bin/getdoc.cgi?9704091540180100|access-date=2010-04-22|volume=31|issue=1|pages=25–31}}</ref>

The Italian sparrow's breeding population is believed to comprise 5 to 10 million pairs, 750,000 to 900,000 of which are estimated to live in urban areas. It has a population density of 58 to 160 pairs per square kilometer.<ref name="Dinetti"/> Up to the mid-1990s, its population increased steadily, probably due to increased urbanisation.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Massa|first1=B.|last2=Milone|first2=M.|last3=Groselj|first3=P.|year=1997|contribution=Italian Sparrow|title=The EBCC Atlas of European Breeding Birds: Their Distribution and Abundance|editor1=Hagemeĳer, W. J. M. |editor2=Blair, M. J.|publisher=T. & A. D. Poyser|location=London|isbn=978-0-85661-091-2|title-link=The EBCC Atlas of European Breeding Birds}}</ref> Between 2000 and 2005, the Italian sparrow's population in Italy declined by 27.1 percent, mirroring the declines of the house sparrow throughout Europe. From 1998 to 2008, urban populations declined by about 50 percent. A study of the Italian sparrow's status listed a large number of potential causes for the Italian sparrow's decline, including shortages of insect food, agricultural intensification, and reductions of green areas.<ref name="Dinetti">{{cite journal|author=Dinetti, Marco|journal=Avocetta|volume=32|year=2008|title=I passeri ''Passer'' spp.: da "problematici" a specie di interesse conservazionistico|pages=61–68|url=http://www.ciso-coi.org/avoxvol32.htm|access-date=2010-04-20|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100505141127/http://www.ciso-coi.org/avoxvol32.htm|archive-date=2010-05-05|url-status=usurped}}</ref> The Italian sparrow is among the most common birds in Italian cities, but other species, including the [[European goldfinch]], are more common.<ref>{{cite journal|journal=Bird Census News|volume=20|issue=2|url=http://www.ebcc.info/wpimages/video/BCN_20_2.pdf|title=Urban Ornithological Atlases in Italy|author1=Fraissinet, Maurizio|author2=Dinetti, Marco|access-date=2010-04-27|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923232754/http://www.ebcc.info/wpimages/video/BCN_20_2.pdf|archive-date=2015-09-23|url-status=dead}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
[[File:Passer italiae MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.208.jpg|thumb|Eggs]]The Italian sparrow's behaviour is similar in many ways to that of the house sparrow. It is a social bird, which feeds mostly on [[seed predation|seeds]] and insects.<ref name="Modena">{{cite journal|journal=Atti della Società dei Naturalisti e Matematici di Modena|year=1889|volume=VIII|title=Elenco degli ucceli Modenese (Continuazione)|author=Picaglia, L.|language=it|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bkoXAAAAYAAJ&q=passer+italiae&pg=RA2-PA21|access-date=2010-04-20}}</ref><ref name="Dinetti"/>

It is mostly sedentary, but it wanders to some extent outside its breeding season. These wanderings are mostly local, but they may extend into southern France. Similarly, the house sparrow sometimes occurs as a winter visitor in northern Italy.<ref>{{harvnb|Summers-Smith|1963| p=117}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Gustin, Marco |author2=Sorace, Alberto|year=2002|title=Autumn movements of Italian Sparrows ''Passer italiae'' in central Italy|journal=Ringing & Migration|volume=21|issue=1|pages=1–4|url=http://www.bto.org/ringing/rmj/rmj-abstracts21-1.htm|access-date=2010-04-20|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100315010020/http://www.bto.org/ringing/rmj/rmj-abstracts21-1.htm|archive-date=15 March 2010|doi=10.1080/03078698.2002.9674270|doi-access=free|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

Besides intergrading with the Spanish and house sparrows, the Italian sparrow has been recorded hybridising with the Eurasian tree sparrow.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Gustin, Marco |author2=Sorace, Alberto |author3=Cecere, Jacodo |title=Plumage and biometrics of two hybrids Italian sparrow ''Passer italiae'' X tree sparrow ''Passer montanus'' in central Italy|journal=Alauda|issn=0002-4619|year=2003|volume=71|issue=3|pages=375–376}}</ref> The eggs of the Italian sparrow do not seem to differ from those of the house sparrow.<ref>{{harvnb|Ogilvie-Grant|1912| p=205}}</ref><ref>{{harvnb|Jourdain|1906| p=92}}</ref> Broods may contain two to eight eggs, with an average of about 5.2.<ref>{{harvnb|Anderson|2006|p=161}}</ref>

== Relationships with humans ==
In most of its range, the Italian sparrow is an abundant and familiar bird of houses. It has been one of the wild birds most commonly consumed as food in Italy.<ref>{{cite journal|first=M. F. M.|last=Meiklejohn|journal=Proceedings of the Nutrition Society|year=1962|volume=21|pmid=14472204|pages=80–83|doi=10.1079/PNS19620014|title=Wild birds as human food|doi-access=free}}</ref> Portrayals of the Italian sparrow or one of its relatives have been found at [[Pompeii]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.nga.gov/exhibitions/2008/pompeii/courtyard.shtm|title=Garden with colonnade (peristyle)|work=Pompeii and the Roman Villa: Art and Culture around the Bay of Naples|publisher=National Gallery of Art|access-date=2010-04-20|archive-date=2010-06-02|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100602052411/http://www.nga.gov/exhibitions/2008/pompeii/courtyard.shtm|url-status=dead}}</ref> Like the house sparrow, the Italian sparrow is considered a [[biological indicator]] of pollution.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Gragnaniello, S. |author2=Fulgione, D. |author3=Milone, M. |author4=Soppelsa, O. |author5=Cacace, P. |author6=Ferrara, L. |date=June 2001|title=Sparrows as Possible Heavy-Metal Biomonitors of Polluted Environments|journal=Bulletin of Environmental Contamination and Toxicology|pmid=11353373|volume=66|issue=6|pages=719–726|doi=10.1007/s001280068|bibcode=2001BuECT..66..719G |s2cid=8789890 }}</ref><gallery mode="packed" heights="200">
File:Italian Sparrow, 00183 Rome, Metropolitan City of Rome Capital, Italia imported from iNaturalist photo 333353428.jpg|Italian sparrow eating pizza, in [[Rome]]
File:Passer flying Pompeii.jpg|A fresco at Pompeii depicting an Italian sparrow or a relative
</gallery>

[[sv:Gråsparv#Italiensk sparv]]

zh.extract:
義大利麻雀（学名：Passer italiae）是雀科麻雀属一种。长期以来意大利麻雀是否为麻雀属的一个独立物种存在争议。2011年发现的遗传学证据表明，意大利麻雀是家麻雀和西班牙麻雀的杂交种，而且意大利麻雀和西班牙麻雀之间已经不再继续杂交繁殖。
